text: We present the case of a sixty-year-old obese woman, subjected to Hartman procedure for acute diverticulitis.
During the induction of pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon observes the absence of adhesions and identifies an incarcerated peristomal hernia.
The surgeon begins the reduction of the peristomal hernia, manipulating the herniated tissue to reposition it within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon begins the mobilization of the splenic flexure, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to facilitate further surgical access.
It's important to find a correct plan. The best solution is to go to virgin field.
During the previous operation the splenic flexure was not mobilized, so the planes at this level are good.
The surgeon prepares to cut the inferior mesenteric vein and the left colic artery, focusing on these vascular structures within the surgical field.
It is not important to search for the inferior mesenteric artery at the origin.
It is not fundamental to isolate the ureter.
The surgeon completes the lateral to medial mobilization of the descending colon, visibly freeing the colon from its attachments and allowing for further manipulation.
The surgeon detaches the stoma, and the pneumoperitoneum is suspended, indicating a change in intra-abdominal pressure.
The head of the circular stapler is inserted into the colon to prepare for the anastomosis.
The surgeon inspects the pelvis, which is visibly free from adhesions, ensuring a clear surgical field for further procedures.
The surgeon uses the handpiece of the circular stapler to expose the wall of the rectum, without isolating the rectum itself.
The surgeon identifies the vagina and carefully moves it away from the surgical field, ensuring clear access to the targeted area.
The surgeon completes the operation by performing a mechanical circular anastomosis, connecting the colon using a circular stapler.
